metadata.name: Red Sea / Houthi Shipping Attacks
metadata.description: Houthi rebel forces in Yemen began attacking commercial vessels transiting the Red Sea and Bab el-Mandeb strait in response to the Israel-Gaza conflict. The attacks forced major container lines (Maersk, MSC, CMA CGM, Hapag-Lloyd) to reroute around the Cape of Good Hope, adding approximately 3,500 nautical miles and 10+ days to Asia-Europe voyages. Container shipping rates surged 200-300%, and the disruption cascaded into port congestion, schedule unreliability, and manufacturing delays globally.

recovery_milestones.description: First Houthi attacks on commercial vessels in Red Sea
Major shipping lines announce Red Sea transit suspension and Cape rerouting
US/UK launch Operation Prosperity Guardian; container rates spike 200%+
Container rates peak at ~300% above pre-crisis; port congestion builds
Schedule disruptions cascade; European manufacturing reports delays
Market adapts to longer routes; rates stabilize at elevated levels
Situation remains fluid; most carriers continue Cape routing

lessons_learned: Maritime chokepoints remain highly vulnerable to asymmetric threats; a non-state actor with anti-ship missiles can disrupt 15% of global trade.
Cape of Good Hope rerouting absorbs the immediate safety risk but creates systemic capacity reduction, as the same number of ships cover longer routes with fewer annual rotations.
Container shipping rate volatility (+200-300%) demonstrates how thin the margin between adequate and insufficient global shipping capacity truly is.
Companies with nearshored or diversified supply chains experienced significantly less disruption than those dependent on Asia-to-Europe maritime lanes.
The Red Sea crisis, combined with the 2021 Suez blockage and pandemic-era port congestion, has accelerated investment in supply chain resilience, dual-sourcing, and digital control towers.
